Observation statistic - computing¶

Statistics can be computed in two different contexts:

  • when feeding the database, data is read from ODS files and temporary (or possibly final) results are computed from variable coming from the files
  • when extracting data from the database to compute final statistics to plot, this is merely an aggregation of temporary results.

The obsstat directive¶

The obsstat directive instructs the system to compute statistics from ODS files, using a set of keywords to specify what is requested. The results of the computations are sent to a storage handler, which by default is an SQL database, but other handlers are available such as text files. The users can write and specificy their own handlers.

Here is a basic example (example 1):

# imports the needed modules to compute statistics
from gmaopy.modules.obsstat import *

d = obsstat(
    # ODS file description
    source = 'oper',                                           # default
    type = 'ob',
    expver = 'e561_tst_02',
    ignore_missing_files = False,                              # default
    # statistic description
    date = Dates(2011020100,2011020118,6),
    domain = ['global','n.hem','s.hem'],
    statistic = ['count'],
    observation = [
        observation(
            usage = 'used',                                    # default
            variable = ['oma','omf','xvec'],
            statistic = ['count','mean','rms','esigo','esigb','normcost'],
            kx = 220,
            kt = [4,5],
        ),
        observation(
            usage = 'unused',
            variable = 'oma',
            kx = 220,
            kt = [4,5],
        ),
        observation(
            usage = 'passive',
            variable = 'oma',
            kx = 220,
            kt = [4,5],
        ),
    ],
    # data storage description (database, text file)
    storage = 'dbstorer',                                     # default
    database = 'ob_ops',
    overwrite = False,                                        # default
)

The lines marked “# default” can be omitted since we specify the default value, they were inserted to be introduced. The meaning of the different keywords can be found in the documentation for the obsstat. However, let us clarify their importance and meaning:

  • the keywords source, type, expver and date are indispensable to locate the ODS files to read. The keyword ignore_missing_files indicates to ignore if an ODS file is not found and keep computing. By default obsstat fails if a file is missing.
  • the keywords storage, database (aliased to output_file) and overwrite affect where the results go, either an SQL database, or a text file. By default obsstat fails if either the statistics computed already exist the specified database or if a text file exists. You can set overwrite to True to overwrite the data. It is advised to keep this flag to False and only set it to True if you are sure about what is happening. The command spy_doc databases provides you with a list of system defined and user defined databases.
  • the keywords date, domain, variable, level (aliased to channel for satellite instruments), kx and kt determine how to extract and filter data from the ODS file to compute statistics on.
  • the keyword statistic defines a list of statistics to be computed and stored.

Please note that in this example, the keyword statistics appears at two different levels:

  • in the body of obsstat
  • in the body of observation

This is because it is required at both levels and it is convenient to use it this way as the only statistic we request for passive and unused data is count. The statistic is inherited by the observations directives.

The directive observation needs to be repeated three times for the three type of data requested: used, passive and unused. The keywords kx and kt cannot be specified in the body of obsstat so they need to be repeated. We can see that it can become cumbersome to define a complex observation statistic computation. In order to help with this, a file can be specified, like in this computation of scores for observation impacts (example 2):

# imports the needed modules to compute statistics
from gmaopy.modules.obsstat import *

obsstat(
    # ODS file  description
    source = 'oper',                                   # default
    type = 'im',
    expver = 'e562p5_fp',
    ignore_missing_files = True,
    # statistic description
    date = Dates(2011030100,2011030200,24),
    domain = ['global','n.hem','s.hem','tropics'],
    statistic = ['sum','beneficial'],
    # data storage description (database, text file)
    storage =  'dbstorer',                             # default
    database = 'im_ops',
    overwrite = False,                                 # default
    obs = [
        observation(
            # this file describes all the kx, kt combinations we want to use
            filename = ['summary.def'],
            variable = 'xvec',
        ),
    ],
)

In this case, the file summary.def we use looks like so:

[aircraft]
kx = 130,131,132,133,134,135,230,231,232,233,234,235
kt = 4,5,44

[aqua_airs]
kx = 49
kt = 40

[amsre]
kx = 547,548,549
kt = 40

[amsua]
kx = 315,316,317,318,319,300,325
kt = 40

[amsub]
kx = 415,416,417
kt = 40

[ascat_wind]
kx = 290
kt = 4,5

[dropsondes]
kx = 132,232
kt = 4,5,11,44

[goes]
kx = 57,58,59,60,61,62,63
kt = 40

[gpsro]
kx = 4,41,42,722,723,740,741,742,743,744,745,786,820
kt = 88

[hirs]
kx = 5,6,7,8,9,10,11,12,14,15,16,17,18,19,25
kt = 40

[iasi]
kx = 875
kt = 40

[land_sfc]
kx = 181,187,281,287
kt = 4,5,11,33,44

[marine_sfc]
kx = 180,182,183,280,282,284
kt = 4,5,11,33,44

[mhs]
kx = 818,819,825
kt = 40

[modis_wind]
kx = 257,258,259
kt = 4,5

[msu]
kx = 205,206,207,208,209,210,211,212,214
kt = 40

[nexrad_wind]
kx = 224
kt = 4,5

[pibal]
kx = 221
kt = 4,5

[profiler_wind]
kx = 223
kt = 4,5

[radiosondes]
kx = 120,220
kt = 4,5,11,33,44

[satellite_wind]
kx = 241,242,243,245,246,247,248,249,250,251,252,253,254,255,256
kt = 4,5

#[sbuv_ozone]
#kx = 457,458,459,460,461,462,464,465,466,467,468,469
#kt = 21,22

[ssmi_rad]
kx = 708,710,711,713,714,715
kt = 40

[ssu]
kx = 305,306,307,308,309,310,311,312,314
kt = 40

[tmi_rain_rate]
kx = 211
kt = 17

[windsat_wind]
kx = 289
kt = 4,5

This file is identical to the kind of file used for plotting statistics (Observation statistic - plotting). In the file, for each group, the keyword level can be used if you want to restrict the computation to less levels for some particular data types.

The obsstat directive has a few problems to solve in order to carry out the instruction:

  • find the required ODS files
  • read and filter variables
  • do the maths

Find ODS files¶

Many different cases can be encountered regarding the location of ODS files:

    1. they are in a standard location parameterized in the system, for example in the operational system they are normally in /home/dao_ops/exvper/run/.../archive/obs. This is where the system looks for them by default. This is conditioned by specifying source=’oper‘.
    1. they are not in a standard location known by the system but they are stored in a consistent way.
    1. they are just anywhere.

The default locations for each different source of data can be modified by the SemperPy administrator, but each user can define his/her own rules and locations. Please refer to Configuring SemperPy for more information about configuration files. To configure the ODS file location, from a configuration directory, you need to create a file called: <config>/retrieve/source.def where source is the source of data (e.g. oper, exp, aer, odas). Here is an example of the standard configuration for source oper:

[fc]
root_tmpl=/home/dao_ops/<expver>/run/.../archive/forecast
tree_tmpl=Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)/Ddate(@d)/Hdate(@H)
file_tmpl=<expver>.<collection>.date(@Y@m@d_@Hz)+valid(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz).nc4
archive_type=dmf

[an]
root_tmpl=/home/dao_ops/<expver>/run/.../archive/diag
tree_tmpl=Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)
file_tmpl=<expver>.<collection>.date(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz).nc4
archive_type=dmf

[ob]
root_tmpl=/home/dao_ops/<expver>/run/.../archive/obs
tree_tmpl=Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)/Ddate(@d)/Hdate(@H)
file_tmpl=<expver>.diag_<collection>.date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods
archive_type=dmf

[im]
root_tmpl=/archive/dao_ops/GEOS-5_pre_ops/GEOSadas-<d1>_<d2>_<d3>/<expver>/obs
tree_tmpl=Ydate(@Y)/Mdate(@m)/Ddate(@d)/Hdate(@H)
file_tmpl=<expver>.imp3_txe_<collection>.obs.date-9(@Y@m@d_@Hz)+date+24(@Y@m@d_@Hz)-date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods
archive_type=dmf

Each section (between square brackets) is the name of a data type, each section may contain four entries:

  • root_tmpl is the template for the root directory of the data structure
  • tree_tmpl is the directory structure under the root
  • file_tmpl is the template to generate the file name.
  • archive_type defines the type of data storage. The existing types of storage are currently dmf for the dmf archive, in that case it also manages to handle dmget to stage the data first and filesystem indicates the file is available directly. Other types could be created such as ftp or scp.

Within the templates, name between <> are keyword names you can recognise from the obsstat directive. There are two special keywords:

  • date: the date as given to the obsstat directive
  • valid: in the case of forecasts, the valid date (date + timestep).

The date formatting (e.g. @Y@m@d_@H@Mz) is a standard unix (and Python) date formatting which can be found on internet or by issuing the command man strftime in the command line. The formatting normally expects % (e.g. %Y%m%d_%H%Mz), but the % sign is interpreted in a special way by Python in that kind of file, so instead of having to go through some heaving character escaping, you can use the character @ instead. Please note that the keywords date and valid accept arithemtic calculations:

date-9(@Y@m@d_@Hz)  # date expressed in hours so date - 9 hours
date+24(@Y@m@d_@Hz) # date expressed in hours so date + 24 hours

For a research experiment, the definition in a file called /home/me/semperpy_config/retrieve/exp.def for observation impact ODS files could be:

[im]
root_tmpl = /discover/nobackup/dmerkova/ObsImpact/data/<expver>/obs/
tree_tmpl = Ydate-9(@Y)/Mdate-9(@m)/Ddate-9(@d)/Hdate-9(@H)
file_tmpl = <expver>.imp3_txe_<collection>.obs.date-9(@Y@m@d_@Hz)+date+24(@Y@m@d_@Hz)-date(@Y@m@d_@Hz).ods
archive_type = filesystem

You will note that the naming convention of impact files in research is different from the convention used in operations.

The path to the configuration structure need to be set:

setenv SEMPERPY_CONFIG /home/me/semperpy_config:${SEMPERPY_CONFIG}
or
export SEMPERPY_CONFIG=/home/me/semperpy_config:${SEMPERPY_CONFIG}

This covers points (a) and (b). For point (c), the easiest way to tell obsstat is to include the information in the body of the directive, (example 3):

# imports the needed modules to compute statistics
from gmaopy.modules.obsstat import *

obsstat(
    # ODS file  description
    source = 'oper',                                   # default
    type = 'im',
    expver = 'e562p5_fp',
    ignore_missing_files = True,
    root_tmpl = './',
    tree_tmpl = '',
    archive_type = 'filesystem',
    # statistic description
    date = Dates(2011030100,2011030200,24),
    domain = ['global','n.hem','s.hem','tropics'],
    statistic = ['sum','beneficial'],
    # data storage description (database, text file)
    storage =  'dbstorer',                             # default
    database = 'im_ops',
    overwrite = False,                                 # default
    obs = [
        observation(
            # this file describes all the kx, kt combinations we want to use
            filename = ['summary.def'],
            variable = 'xvec',
        ),
    ],
)

This means that all the ODS files are in the current directory. Specifying root_tmpl = ‘’ means that there is no directory tree, not specifying file_tmpl means that the files have their original names. Both when specifying these keywords in the body of the obsstat directive or a configuration file, if a keyword is not specified, it is inherited from the SemperPy system configuration file.

Read and filter variables¶

As mentioned above, the keywords date, domain, variable, level, kx and kt specify the actual observations the user wants to select to compute statistics. If level is not specified, it is understood as all available levels for each type of data*.

The obsstat directive splits the data into usable chunks to be able to save results which can later be aggregated for plotting. The only dimension which is fixed at compute time is domain. Once statistics are computed for a specific domain, not geographical aggregation can be done. If the user needs to plot statistics for a new geographical domain, he / she will have to go back to obsstat to populate the database with results for that new domain. All other dimensions are slit to the smalled chunk, each statistic is store for one particular:

  • date,
  • domain,
  • variable,
  • level,
  • kt,
  • kx,
  • usage

All these dimensions can be aggregated at plotting time. Please note that for each statistic, the following keywords are also used:

  • source,
  • type,
  • expver

That way different data from different experiments / source / type can be stored in the database without any risk of overwriting. If the obsstat directive fails because it’s going to overwrite existing data in the database, take the time to think about whether it is normal that the data is already present in the database or whether some keywords need changing.

Please note that some statistics require specific variables from the ODS file. For example the Normalized cost requires either oma or omf (specified by the user) and xvec. Even if the user does not specify xvec in the list of variables, if will be extracted anyway because obsstat knows that is is required for normcost.

Do the maths¶

Once all the chunks of data have been extracted from the ODS files, the obsstat presents each chunk of data to the statistics system following each statistic requirement (needed variables). Each statistic object is is provided with the data and a storer (either text file, SQL database or others or all of them).

Please refer to Add your own statistic for more information.

  • Observation statistic - computing

      • Read and filter variables
      • Do the maths
